content:
@Danilo Krummrich

Hi Danilo, I see that you have changed from &mut T to &Opaque<T> in the ioctl macro.

We should probably dereference that instead of handing the Opaque to the drivers to avoid the proliferation of unsafe and the handling of raw pointers at the driver level. This comes at a cost of two extra memcpy's per ioctl.

My proposal is:

                            let data = unsafe {
                                &*(raw_data as *const $crate::types::Opaque<$crate::uapi::$struct>)
                            };

                            // Copy into our stack and pass that to the drivers
                            // so they get a &mut T, instead of &Opaque<T>
                            let args  = unsafe {*data.get()};

                            // SAFETY: This is just the DRM file structure
                            let file = unsafe { $crate::drm::File::as_ref(raw_file) };

                            match $func(dev, &mut args, file) {
                                Err(e) => e.to_errno(),
                                Ok(i) => i.try_into()
                                            .unwrap_or($crate::error::code::ERANGE.to_errno()),
                            }

                            // Writeback the updates, if any.
                            core::ptr::write(data.get(), args);

WDYT?

How do you guarantee that the data behind data.get() is valid? I think we can only ever guarantee that if we only allow uAPI structures that are impossible for userspace to create invalid instances from for Rust.

But even if we do that, my reasoning was that it's not worth at all. uAPIs typically pass pointers through u64 fields to the kernel, which must be handled by drivers anyways.

I think the correct answer is to provide generic helper infrastructure, to make it easy for drivers to generate the corresponding uAPI abstractions. See what I do in nova-drm [1], I think we should generalize that.

[1] https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/drm/nova/-/blob/staging/nova-drm/drivers/gpu/drm/nova/uapi.rs?ref_type=heads
IIUC the code correctly, I assume we need a proc-macro for that? Since we need the AST to know which functions to generate

How do you guarantee that the data behind data.get() is valid?

That is already a problem with the currently solution IIUC?
Where do you see this problem with the current solution?

Danilo Krummrich said:

Where do you see this problem with the current solution?

IIUC:

The current solution builds an Opaque<T> from a raw pointer, i.e.:

                            // SAFETY: This is just the ioctl argument, which hopefully has the
                            // right type (we've done our best checking the size).
                            let data = unsafe { &mut *(raw_data as *mut $crate::uapi::$struct) };

So the opaque is only as valid as is the raw pointer it wraps.

The current solution then passes &Opaque<T> to the drivers, which now have to call .get() to be able to actually access the uapi data, so the whole thing only works if we assume that .get() returns valid data. It's just that this responsibility now lays with the driver.
Ah, maybe we're relying on the MaybeUnit<T> to save us here? In which case I see your point
Yes, that's exactly the purpose of Opaque, to get us "rid of all the usual assumptions that Rust has for a value". :slight_smile:
And yes, the responsibility is on the driver. Unfortunately, it has to be -- none of the generic layers know the type and its semantics.
That's why I said we should generalize what [1] does and yes, I think proc-macros would be a great option to describe the uAPI at a higher level to generate the uAPI abstraction.

How do you guarantee that the data behind data.get() is valid? I think we can only ever guarantee that if we only allow uAPI structures that are impossible for userspace to create invalid instances from for Rust.

Don't we effectively already have that requirement (at a uAPI design level, nothing specific to Rust)?
I would expect that uapi data structures are always valid for any sequence of bytes, so we could just use them without opaque.
Hm, I was looking for things like “bool” or similar where you could have an invalid byte pattern
But I remembered that this is taken into account in C too, so I don’t think there’s anywhere where bool is used on the uAPI
This leaves a bunch of u8 to u64, sized arrays and pointers
I don’t think there’s any particular requirements on pointers either
There shouldn't be pointer types, pointers are passed as u64.
However, drivers would still need an abstraction for dealing with those pointers.
Right but this is a different thing, right? We’re initially discussing the Opaque and whether it’s really needed
That is true.
Anyways, I tried to remember why I wasn't convinced that uAPI headers only ever contain types that in Rust are valid for any sequence of bytes.
And I think my concern was mostly about unions.
But I'd rather avoid them in uAPIs anyways...

Danilo Krummrich said:

Anyways, I tried to remember why I wasn't convinced that uAPI headers only ever contain types that in Rust are valid for any sequence of bytes.

what is special about unions for this particular case?

I think I thought about something like [1], even though we shouldn't do something like that.

Anyways, if we can ensure that we never get undefined behavior without Opaque, that's great, we should do it then.

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v6.14.6/source/include/uapi/drm/vmwgfx_drm.h#L1107

hmm, this appears to be valid for any sequence of bytes, no?

the enum will be converted to an integer, so there is no way to have an invalid variant or something like this, and the struct is just two integers
also, rust unions do not have a discriminant, so they should be 1:1 compatible with C from that perspective as well
yeah, I am starting to think that we should be good here even with unions
Btw @Beata Michalska , this conversation is probably interesting to you
